title: How AI Learns
subtitle: Training data, patterns, and your first machine learning experiment

diagram.alt: The machine learning training loop: collect data, label it, train the model, evaluate, improve, and deploy

overview: Yesterday we discovered that AI is already everywhere. Today we go inside the machine. We will explore how AI actually learns — through data, patterns, and repetition — and then you will train your very own AI model using Google's Teachable Machine. No coding required.
By the end of today, you will understand the full loop of machine learning: collect data → train the model → test it → improve it. This loop is the foundation of almost every AI system on the planet.
objectives: Explain the machine learning training loop in plain language
Understand what training data, features, labels, and models are
Experience the "sorting exercise" to feel what training data means
Train a real image classifier using Google Teachable Machine
Test the model's accuracy and understand why it makes mistakes

concepts.heading: The Machine Learning Training Loop
body: Every machine learning system follows the same basic cycle. Understanding this loop unlocks your ability to think about any AI system:
bullets: Collect Data: Gather thousands (or millions) of examples.
Label the Data: Tell the AI what each example is (e.g., "cat" or "dog").
Train the Model: The AI finds mathematical patterns that link examples to labels.
Evaluate: Test the model on new examples it has never seen.
Improve: Add more data, fix mistakes, retrain — repeat until good enough.
Deploy: Release the model to be used in the real world.

concepts.heading: What Is a Feature?
body: A feature is any measurable characteristic the AI uses to make decisions. For example, when recognising a cat:

concepts.heading: Types of Machine Learning
body: There is more than one way for an AI to learn. Here are the four big families:

rows: You provide labelled examples. Most common type. Used for: image recognition, spam filters, voice assistants.
No labels — AI finds patterns on its own. Used for: customer grouping, anomaly detection.
AI learns by trial and error, rewarded for good actions. Used for: games, robotics, self-driving cars.
Take a pre-trained model and fine-tune it for a new task. Powers most modern AI apps — much faster than training from scratch.

concepts.heading: Overfitting and Underfitting — Why AI Fails
body: Two of the most common problems in machine learning:

rows: The AI memorises the training examples too perfectly, and fails on any new data. Like a student who memorises textbook answers but can't answer a slightly different question on the exam.
The AI hasn't learned enough. It performs badly even on training data. Like a student who barely studied and gets everything wrong.

text: The Golden Rule of ML: Good AI generalises. It learns patterns that work on new, unseen data — not just data it has memorised.
This is why testing on separate "held-out" data is critical before deploying any AI system.

activities.name: The Human Sorting Game
intro: In your group you will get a shuffled stack of animal picture cards. You are about to become a machine learning model — by hand!
steps: Round 1: Without talking, each person sorts the cards into piles — any way you like.
Round 2: Reveal the "correct labels." Re-sort the cards using those labels.
Discuss: What features did you use to sort? How did having labels change your approach?
Debrief: What features did you use to tell cats from dogs? What made it hard?
Debrief: If someone gave you 10,000 of these cards to sort by yourself, would you use the same method? What would you change?
Debrief: What if some of the labels on the cards were wrong? How would that affect your learning?
Debrief: Imagine the AI only saw white cats and orange cats. What would happen when it met a black cat for the first time?
facilitator: Print 40–60 animal images (mix of cats, dogs, birds, fish) on cards. Shuffle them.
Divide students into groups of 4. Each group receives a shuffled set of cards.
Round 1: Without talking, each student sorts the cards into piles — any way they like.
Round 2: Reveal the "correct labels." Students re-sort using the labels.
Discussion: What features did you use to sort? How did having labels change your approach?
Connect to ML: "What you just did manually is exactly what machine learning does automatically — but with millions of examples and millions of features simultaneously."

activities.name: Teachable Machine: Train Your First AI
intro: Now you will train a real image classifier — no coding required. You will teach an AI to recognise three hand gestures: thumbs up, peace sign, and fist.
steps: Step 1 — Collect Your Data: Rename "Class 1" to "Thumbs Up." Click "Webcam" and hold your thumb up — record at least 100 images.
Rename "Class 2" to "Peace Sign" — record another 100 images.
Click "Add a class" → rename to "Fist" — record another 100 images.
Step 2 — Train: Click the big "Train Model" button — watch it learn! After training, the "Preview" panel activates automatically.
Step 3 — Test: Try each gesture in front of the webcam — does it get them right? Move your hand, change the lighting, use your other hand — does it still work?
Try tricking it — show a gesture halfway between two classes. What does it predict?
Challenge: If your model makes mistakes, add more training images from different angles and lighting, then retrain. Did adding more data improve accuracy? Record your findings.

table.caption: Student Data Collection Table — record your results here
rows: Number of images per class (first attempt)
Where did your model fail? (describe the situation)
What did you change to improve it?
What would happen if you added a 4th class? Try it!

facilitator: Visit teachablemachine.withgoogle.com — no account needed, works in any browser with a webcam.
Walk students through the interface briefly before they start.
Suggested project: Train the AI to recognise 3 hand gestures (thumbs up, peace sign, fist).

reflection: Describe the machine learning loop in your own words, as if explaining it to a 10-year-old.
What did you discover when you tried to trick your Teachable Machine model? What does this tell you about AI?
When your model made mistakes, what was usually the cause? Too little data? Bad angle? Wrong lighting?
Think of a real-world AI system (e.g. Spotify recommendations, Google Photos face recognition). What training data do you think it was trained on?
The AI you trained today only took a few minutes. Real AI systems can take weeks of training on thousands of computers. Why do you think that is?

vocab.definition: The examples used to teach an AI.
The correct answer attached to a training example.
A measurable characteristic the AI uses to make decisions.
The mathematical structure the AI builds from training data.
Memorising training data too closely, failing on new data.
Percentage of correct predictions on test data.
A layered mathematical structure loosely inspired by the brain.

quiz.q: What is the correct order of the machine learning training loop?
options: Collect data → Label → Train → Evaluate → Improve → Deploy
Train → Collect data → Deploy → Label → Evaluate → Improve
Label → Deploy → Train → Collect data → Evaluate → Improve
Deploy → Improve → Evaluate → Train → Label → Collect data
quiz.explain: First you collect examples, then label them, then train a model on the patterns, evaluate it on new data, improve it, and finally deploy it to the real world.

quiz.q: Which of these is a "feature" an AI might use to recognise a cat?
options: The word "cat" printed on the photo
How many people have seen the photo
The file size of the image
quiz.explain: A feature is a measurable characteristic the AI uses to decide, like pointy ears, fur pattern, or eye shape. It learns which features reliably predict "cat."

quiz.q: Unsupervised learning uses labelled examples to learn.
quiz.explain: False. Unsupervised learning has NO labels — the AI finds patterns on its own. It's supervised learning that uses labelled examples.

quiz.q: A model gets every training example perfect but fails badly on brand-new photos. What is the problem called?
quiz.explain: This is overfitting — the AI memorised the training data too perfectly, like a student who memorises textbook answers but can't handle a slightly different exam question.

quiz.q: The Golden Rule of ML says a good AI should memorise its training data as exactly as possible.
quiz.explain: False. The Golden Rule is that good AI generalises — it learns patterns that work on new, unseen data, not just data it has memorised. That's why we test on separate held-out data.
